sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Jacqueline, presents with anxiety regarding her recent blood work results indicating hepatitis C.
She reports feeling fine and has no prior knowledge of having hepatitis C.
She denies any history of IV drug use in the past 15 years but admits to previous heavy alcohol use, currently drinking about 1 beer a day.
She also admits to smoking 1-2 cigarettes a day.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of hypertension, diabetes, and depression.
Her family history includes hypertension, diabetes, and depression.
Review of systems: The patient reports feeling tired at the end of the day, despite getting enough sleep.
She denies any other symptoms.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient appears in no distress and no jaundice on skin exam.
The heart exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm with a 2/6 systolic murmur, which has not changed from last year.
The lungs are clear with no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
The abdomen is soft with normal bowel sounds and no hepatosplenomegaly.
The musculoskeletal exam is unremarkable.
Test Results: The patient's recent blood work results show a positive HCV antibody test, elevated AST (39), normal ALT, and normal total bilirubin.
The liver panel results are consistent with hepatitis C.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a confirmed diagnosis of hepatitis C, a viral infection affecting the liver, likely acquired years ago.
Problem list: Hepatitis C; active; suspected liver disease; unknown.
Plan:
I prescribed [NOT MENTIONED].
I ordered additional blood work to confirm the diagnosis, including HCV RNA and HCV genotype.
I also ordered an ultrasound to determine the severity of the patient's liver disease.
I will use the results to determine the next steps in treatment.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in three weeks to review the results of the additional blood work and ultrasound.
